perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall, during the term of this Agreement and for a period of five (5) years after the termination of this Agreement, use reasonable care to maintain the confidentiality of Confidential Information and to limit its disclosure to only: (a) those of its directors, officers, employees, agents or professional advisors as have a need to know that Confidential Information in order that the objectives of the Discussions and Negotiations can be achieved; and (b) to any of its affiliates or subsidiaries so long as the affiliate or subsidiary understands and agrees that the Confidential Information is being provided subject to all terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall, during the term of this Agreement and for a period of one (1) year after the termination of this Agreement, use reasonable care to maintain the confidentiality of Confidential Information and to limit its disclosure to only: (a) those of its directors, officers, employees, agents or professional advisors as have a need to know that Confidential Information in order that the objectives of the Discussions and Negotiations can be achieved; and (b) to any of its affiliates or subsidiaries so long as the affiliate or subsidiary understands and agrees that the Confidential Information is being provided subject to all terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under applicable laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Protection of trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Many trade secrets retain value and require protection for longer than one year. Limiting the confidentiality period to one year undermines the protection afforded to trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that a one-year period may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 USC 1836, discusses civil proceedings related to the protection of trade secrets. This implies that trade secrets have legal protections and the one-year confidentiality period weakens this protection, creating a contradiction because the reduced period could undermine the legal safeguards afforded to trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Legally Required Disclosure. If the Receiving Party becomes legally compelled to disclose any Confidential Information, the Receiving Party shall: (a) provide the Disclosing Party with prompt written notice so that the Disclosing Party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement; and (b) delay such disclosure as long as reasonably possible (without incurring liability for failure to make such a disclosure) to permit the Disclosing Party to seek a protection order and to allow for consultation with the Disclosing Party with respect to any reasonable alternatives to the required disclosure and with respect to the content of that disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Legally Required Disclosure. If the Receiving Party becomes legally compelled to disclose any Confidential Information, the Receiving Party shall: (a) provide the Disclosing Party with notice only after the disclosure has occurred; and (b) immediately proceed with the disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the requirement for prior notice and the opportunity for the Disclosing Party to seek a protective order, potentially violating legal procedures.
perturbation.law_explanation: The changed text prevents the disclosing party from seeking a protective order under FRCP 26(c), which allows parties to seek court orders to protect confidential information from being disclosed in litigation. By mandating immediate disclosure without prior notice, the Receiving Party prevents the Disclosing Party from exercising their legal right to protect their confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the process outlined in FRCP 26(c) as it prevents the disclosing party from seeking a protective order by mandating immediate disclosure without prior notice. This action effectively removes the Disclosing Party's right to protect their confidential information as it is a direct violation of legal procedure. The original text required prior notice so the Disclosing Party could seek remedy, the changed text eliminates this right.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Return of Confidential Information. The Receiving Party shall, at its own expense, promptly return to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information, including all written information, whether originals or copies of documents, records, summaries, analyses, and notes, and all materials in its possession that contain Confidential Information, regardless of who authored or generated such written information or materials, to the Disclosing Party immediately upon their request. If any of the Confidential Information is destroyed prior to the request, an adequate response to a return request of that Confidential Information by the Disclosing Party will be a written certificate, executed by an authorized officer of the Receiving Party, certifying that the relevant Confidential Information was destroyed. Additionally, the Receiving Party shall at the same time remove all digital representations, in any form, that contain any Confidential Information, from all electronic storage media in its possession or under its control.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Return of Confidential Information. The Receiving Party is not obligated to return or destroy any Confidential Information upon request. The Receiving Party may retain Confidential Information indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to return or destroy confidential information contradicts standard trade secret protection practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) aims to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. By allowing the Receiving Party to retain Confidential Information indefinitely, the modified text creates a risk of misappropriation and contradicts the UTSA's objective of protecting trade secret holders' rights.

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the purpose of trade secret protection by removing the Receiving Party's obligation to return or destroy confidential information, explicitly stating they may retain it indefinitely. This directly undermines the protection of trade secrets, as the original text ensured the Disclosing Party maintained control over its confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 14. Notices. Any notice, request or consent which may or is required to be given pursuant to this Agreement will be in writing and will be delivered or faxed as follows: if to Stuart Energy, to Stuart Energy Systems Corporation, 5101 Orbitor Drive, Mississauga, Ontario, L4W 4V1, Attention: R. Randall MacEwen, Vice President, Corporate Development, General Counsel and Corporate Secretary, facsimile number 905.282.7701, or if to Hydrogenics, to Hydrogenics Corporation, 5985 McLaughlin Road, Mississauga, Ontario L5R 1B8, Attention: Jonathan Lundy, Vice President, Strategic Development and Corporate Secretary, facsimile number 905.361.3626 or to such other address as the relevant party may from time to time advise by notice in writing given in accordance with this Section 14. The time and date of receipt of any such notice, request or consent will be deemed to be the time and date of the actual receipt thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. Notices. Any notice, request or consent which may or is required to be given pursuant to this Agreement will be valid only if sent via carrier pigeon. No other form of communication is acceptable.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting notice to only carrier pigeon delivery is commercially unreasonable and likely unenforceable, conflicting with standard contract law principles regarding reasonable notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 237 outlines conditions where a party's duties are discharged following a breach. Requiring notice only via carrier pigeon introduces an unreasonable and impractical condition, potentially preventing effective notice and discharging obligations when proper notice could not be given. This contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing inherent in contract law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly restricts acceptable notice to "carrier pigeon" only, deeming "no other form of communication" acceptable. This creates an inconsistency by establishing an unreasonable and impractical method for providing notice. The Law Explanation says this introduces an "unreasonable and impractical condition, potentially preventing effective notice and discharging obligations when proper notice could not be given." This goes against the implied duty of good faith. No direct snippets were available to cite, but the extreme unreliability of the pigeon delivery method makes it a clear violation. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition says that 'a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal', which applies here.
